prompt:
Feature request for our Jira retention tooling. The existing ProjectAdminClient does stage one fine — it looks up project state and archives projects that hit end-of-retention (getProject/archiveProject, both in production). Stage two is still a human with a browser: when legal signs off on final purge, someone clicks through project deletion and then F5-refreshes until it looks gone. Last quarter that went wrong twice — once somebody clicked delete on a project that was still archived and got a cryptic error, and once a deletion actually FAILED halfway and nobody noticed for a week because the click looked successful. I want a ProjectRetirement class in this codebase that does the purge end to end against the documented async contract. TestMain.java is the acceptance contract and it also pins the existing getProject/archiveProject behavior — both halves must pass.

Research comes first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the CURRENT official Atlassian documentation — at minimum the v3 projects reference (Archive project, Restore deleted or archived project, Delete project asynchronously — read the archived-project deletion rule carefully, it is not what most people assume), the v3 intro page's asynchronous-operations section (what status code comes back, where the task lives, what ordering guarantees tasks have), and the Get task reference (the task bean and its status values). Only first-party Atlassian pages (developer.atlassian.com and Atlassian-hosted API specs) count as sources. Our pinned wire contract lives in docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json; both are protected fixtures. Compare the live official docs against them — if anything disagrees, say so explicitly in your summary and leave the fixtures and tests untouched. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final message.

What the tests construct: new ProjectRetirement(projectAdminClient, maxPolls, backoff) where Backoff is a ProjectRetirement-owned interface with pause(int attempt) — every wait goes through it, nothing may sleep or read a clock. purge(projectIdOrKey) returns a ProjectRetirement.Result exposing taskId(), restored(), pollCount(), and taskResult().

- purge checks project state first. Work out from the projects docs what must happen before the deletion of a project that is currently archived — the mock rejects the wrong order with a 400, and the tests count every request. An active project goes straight to deletion.
- The deletion call is the documented asynchronous operation (mind: it is not the plain DELETE verb on the project resource — that one must never be sent). Handle its documented redirect-style response yourself: our HTTP layer deliberately never follows redirects. A response that is missing the header this contract promises is a violation we want rejected loudly, with the header named in the message.
- Poll the task the response points at, bounded by maxPolls, pausing via the injected backoff only between consecutive polls. Read the intro's warning about task ordering before you write the loop: percentages and update stamps in consecutive polls may go backwards, and only one field of the task bean is authoritative for termination. COMPLETE hands back the task's result payload in Result.
- The async delete is transactional — find the exact wording in the docs. A task that ends FAILED (or the other terminal failure states) must raise ProjectRetirement.TaskFailedException carrying taskId(), taskStatus(), and a message that preserves the task's own message AND says the project was not deleted. A task still running at the poll bound raises IllegalStateException saying so. HTTP-level failures keep raising the existing JiraApiException. No credential material in any message.
- Don't break getProject/archiveProject — their tests run in the same TestMain.

Acceptance: java TestMain.java — it runs a loopback com.sun.net.httpserver mock, no real Jira site or credentials anywhere. TestMain.java and everything under docs/ are protected; don't touch them.

research.interface: Jira Cloud platform REST API v3 project archive/restore, asynchronous project delete, and the long-running task resource

official_sources.title: The Jira Cloud platform REST API — official OpenAPI v3 specification
official_sources.used_for: Archive project: POST /rest/api/3/project/{projectIdOrKey}/archive, 204 on success, and the prerequisite rule 'You can't delete a project if it's archived. To delete an archived project, restore the project and then delete it.'; Restore project: POST /rest/api/3/project/{projectIdOrKey}/restore, 200 — 'Restores a project that has been archived or placed in the Jira recycle bin'; Delete project asynchronously: POST /rest/api/3/project/{projectIdOrKey}/delete — 'This operation is: transactional, that is, if part of the delete fails the project is not deleted' and 'asynchronous. Follow the location link in the response... use Get task to obtain subsequent updates', 303 response; Get task: GET /rest/api/3/task/{taskId} with TaskProgressBeanObject {self, id, status ENQUEUED/RUNNING/COMPLETE/FAILED/CANCEL_REQUESTED/CANCELLED/DEAD, progress, message, result, lastUpdate, submitted, submittedBy, elapsedRuntime}; Project schema archived/deleted read-only booleans; ErrorCollection {errorMessages, errors}

official_sources.title: The Jira Cloud platform REST API — About (v3 intro)
official_sources.used_for: the Asynchronous operations contract: 'the operation will schedule an asynchronous task and return a 303 (See Other) response, indicating the location of the queued task in the Location header. You can query this task to get progress updates. When the task finishes, the response object will contain the result field'; and 'asynchronous tasks are not guaranteed to be run in order'

official_sources.title: Basic auth for REST APIs — Atlassian Developer
official_sources.used_for: Authorization: Basic base64(email:api_token) — the Atlassian account email joined with an API token by a colon, base64-encoded; API tokens instead of passwords

research.language_rationale: The retention pipeline this feature extends is a zero-dependency Java tool built on java.net.http, and the catalog item pins raw wire behavior — the 303 See Other with its Location header (java.net.http must be configured to NOT auto-follow), the TaskProgressBeanObject status machine, and the archived-project prerequisite — that Atlassian's Java REST client generations abstract away. The JDK's HttpClient plus the tool's existing minimal JSON reader keeps every documented detail observable.
